title-group.article-title: Central Retinal Artery Occlusion: A Review of Pathophysiological Features and Management
title-group.alt-title: A Review of Pathophysiology and Management

aff.named-content: Lillian S. Wells Department of Neurosurgery

abstract.p: Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) is a form of acute ischemic stroke that results in painless vision loss attributable to retinal infarction. A keen understanding of clinical presentation and underlying pathophysiological features is key to timely intervention and development of new treatment modalities. In CRAO, the time between initial insult to presentation is significant because, analogous to ischemic stroke, the duration of ischemia is inversely related to viable retinal tissue. A major challenge in CRAO is delayed presentation, which reduces the amount of salvageable retina. In addition, imaging techniques to effectively identify a retinal penumbra, or retinal tissue that is reversibly damaged, are not well established. To compile this narrative review, we conducted a systematic search of the PubMed database to identify relevant articles on the pathophysiological features and treatment of CRAO, including reviews, meta‐analyses, clinical studies, observational trials, and randomized trials. The search strategy included the following keywords: central retinal artery occlusion, CRAO, treatment, management, review, meta‐analysis, clinical study, observational trial, and randomized trial. We also searched for ongoing clinical trials related to CRAO on ClinicalTrials.gov. The identified articles and studies were then carefully evaluated for their relevance to the topic and used in compiling this review. Intravenous thrombolysis is a compelling therapeutic approach, with current limited data suggesting early intervention (4.5 hours of symptom onset) results in better patient outcomes. However, ongoing trials assessing and comparing different fibrinolytic agents, routes of administration (venous versus arterial), and timing of intervention will provide further insight on the efficacy of this treatment modality. In parallel, development and testing of imaging techniques aimed at quantifying retinal blood flow and assessing tissue viability could improve risk stratification to guide treatment. These can then be used in conjunction to guide use of conventional therapies, neuroprotectants, and thrombolytics for the management of various CRAO presentations that can be effectively deployed in emergency settings. This article provides a narrative review of pathophysiological features, risk factors, and current and emerging management techniques of CRAO.

list-item.p: Central retinal artery occlusion presents a significant challenge in ophthalmology and stroke management because of its sudden onset and irreversible consequences.

Our review focuses on the current evidence and clinical approach for managing central retinal artery occlusion, while highlighting emerging techniques for early identification and revascularization of the retinal penumbra (eg, optical coherence tomography, retinal functional imaging, and photoacoustic microscopy).

Implementation of novel imaging techniques for early identification, improved thrombolytic therapies, endovascular strategies, and novel neuroprotectants can potentially revolutionize treatment paradigms, significantly improving patient outcomes.

body.p: Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) is an ophthalmic emergency characterized by vision loss attributable to obstruction of blood flow through the central retinal artery (CRA), 1 of the major arteries supplying blood to the eye. CRAO can be categorized into 4 distinct subtypes that can differ in cause, clinical presentation, and treatment. These subtypes include classic nonarteritic CRAO (NA‐CRAO), NA‐CRAO with cilioretinal artery sparing, transient NA‐CRAO, and arteritic CRAO. 1

The cause and pathophysiological features of each subtype of CRAO differ from one another. Most cases of classic NA‐CRAO are caused by an embolus originating from plaques in the internal carotid artery. 2 , 3 In comparison, arteritic CRAO is caused by inflammatory vasculitides and autoimmune disorders, including giant cell arteritis (GCA), polyarteritis nodosa, and systemic lupus erythematosus, in which inflammatory narrowing, occlusion, or rupture of the arteries, like the common trunk of the posterior ciliary artery and CRA, occurs. 4 Some conditions predisposing patients to CRAO include the following: hypercholesterolemia, attributable to plaque formation in the carotid arteries, increasing the risk of embolism to the CRA; carotid artery disease, via embolism, stenosis, and serotonin‐induced vasospasm, which can decrease or prevent blood flow to the CRA; diabetes and arterial hypertension, which impact the integrity of the microvasculature; cardiac conditions, like patent foramen ovale, valvular abnormalities (regurgitation/stenosis), and atrial fibrillation, which can increase the risk of CRAO caused by embolism from the heart; GCA and other vasculitis; and some chronic systemic autoimmune diseases and hypercoagulable states. 1 , 3 , 5 , 6 , 7 , 8 , 9 , 10 , 11 , 12 , 13 Iatrogenic causes include perioperative complications, such as embolization and changes in blood viscosity (and hypercoagulation), especially after head and neck procedures and hemodialysis, respectively. 1 , 8

The incidence of CRAO varies based on demographics and risk factors, but when adjusted for age and sex, the incidence is 1.9 per 100 000 person‐years in the United States, 1.8 per 100 000 person‐years in South Korea, and 2.5 per 100 000 person‐years in Japan. 14 In the population aged >80 years, the incidence increases to 10.1 per 100 000 person‐years. 14 Of all cases of CRAO, it has been shown that 95% are of the nonarteritic subtypes, whereas only 5% are arteritic. 14 One nonmodifiable risk factor for CRAO is age, given its higher prevalence in older people; however, most risk factors are related to conditions causing arterial disease. 1

Many of the risk factors previously discussed also increase the risk for occlusive cardiovascular and cerebrovascular events, so an important step in managing patients with CRAO is identification of an embolic source to prevent additional thromboembolic events. 1 , 14 A meta‐analysis revealed that 30% of patients with CRAO have acute ischemic strokes on magnetic resonance imaging within 7 days of symptom onset. 15 Thus, appropriate workup and management of risk factors for thromboembolism are essential in patients with CRAO. Atrial fibrillation, in particular, has a high incidence in the patient population with CRAO, with almost 50% of patients with CRAO estimated to have new‐onset atrial fibrillation identified after CRAO. 13 Thus, it is likely that thromboemboli from the heart cause a significant proportion of CRAO cases, highlighting the importance of working up patients with CRAO to identify an embolic source to prevent additional ischemic strokes. Beneficial lifestyle modifications, such as plant‐based diets and regular physical activity, can improve risk factors, like dyslipidemia, hypertension, obesity, and diabetes. 14 CRAO is included in the American Heart Association definition of stroke. Thus, patients with CRAO should likely receive long‐term prophylactic antiplatelet therapy with aspirin or ticagrelor, just like patients with ischemic stroke, to prevent future CRAOs or cerebral ischemic strokes. 14

Accurate diagnosis and effective treatment for CRAO are imperative because, despite current knowledge and extensive research efforts, <20% of patients regain visual acuity of the affected eye. 14 Although various treatment modalities have been studied, there are still some misconceptions and controversies when it comes to the efficacy of some widely adopted interventions; in fact, some trials studying the efficacy of tPA (tissue‐type plasminogen activator), a commonly used modality, have shown some success in the treatment of CRAO, whereas others have shown tPA to cause more harm than benefit. 1 , 14 More conservative treatments, like ocular massage, lowering intraocular pressure (IOP), and anterior chamber paracentesis, that aim to dislodge emboli and restore blood flow are also commonly used, but have also shown minimal results. 14 In this narrative review, we provide an in‐depth overview of the current understanding of CRAO and highlight the various existing and forthcoming treatment modalities aimed at improving diagnosis and overall patient outcomes.

sec.title: Pathophysiological Features of CRAO
sec.p: The inner retina, which is made up of the retinal nerve fiber layer, the ganglion cell layer, and the inner plexiform layer, is supplied by the CRA and its branches. After the internal carotid artery emerges from the cavernous sinus, the ophthalmic artery is the first branch of the cerebral segment of internal carotid artery and is the source of the CRA (Figure 1). Inner retinal ischemia comes from retinal artery occlusion. However, a cilioretinal artery is present in approximately one‐third of eyes and frequently supplies the fovea, which is critical for central vision. 16 Instead of the central retinal arterial circulation, the cilioretinal artery develops from the posterior ciliary circulation. 14 , 17 Therefore, in patients with a cilioretinal artery, collateral circulation of the fovea means visual acuity, a measure of central vision, may return to practically normal levels following a CRAO, although peripheral vision will still be significantly impaired. 16

caption.p: A summary of vascular supply to the retina and central retinal artery occlusion pathophysiological features. 18 GCA indicates giant cell arteritis; HLD, Hyperlipidemia; HTN, hypertension; and SLE, systemic lupus erythematosus.

sec.p: The inner retina becomes significantly edematous soon after it is injured. The inner retina atrophies when the edema subsides over time. However, the length of time that the CRA has been occluded is the main factor in determining the extent of retinal damage and the eventual quality of visual outcome in patients. 19 , 20 , 21 The division of the inner retinal circulation (supplied by the CRA) and the external retinal/choroidal circulation (provided by the posterior ciliary circulation) are vital in predicting the pathophysiological features of CRAO. 14 , 21 , 22 Moreover, the collateralization between these 2 vascular supplies to the retina may occur at the optic nerve head and through the pial anastomosis of the central artery. 3 , 14 , 17 Understanding of these physiological features led to the hypothesis that passive oxygen diffusion from the outer to the thin inner retina can occur in CRAO, hence forming the theoretical basis for hyperbaric oxygen therapy as an early intervention to recover dying retinal tissue 3 , 14 , 17 , 20 , 21 , 22 , 23 (see the Conventional Treatment section). The vascular occlusion may start to recannulate over the course of a week following the occlusive event but may take several weeks to completely resolve. 1 However, the retina is extremely sensitive to ischemia and, unfortunately, retinal ganglion cells may be vulnerable to damage and apoptosis at a sooner time point. Although some animal experimental studies show retinal ganglion cell damage within 12 to 15 minutes following artery blockage, other animal studies and some clinical observations have shown that it may take 100 to 240 minutes of occlusion to cause irreversible damage of retinal tissue, suggesting a longer window of viability. 8 , 19 , 24 , 25 , 26 , 27 Accordingly, depending on how long the blood flow has been compromised, the vision loss associated with CRAO can vary and frequently be permanent, with only a limited amount of visual recovery.

In CRAO, the ability of CRA to deliver blood to retina is compromised, resulting in infarction, consistent with ischemic stroke. In clinical variants of CRAO where a cilioretinal artery is present and spared, patients have a better visual prognosis.

In cases of NA‐CRAO, a thromboembolus usually lodges at the CRA's narrowest point, where it pierces the optic nerve's dural sheath. 14 , 28 , 29 , 30 In other instances, an occlusive thrombus in CRA may occur posterior to the lamina cribrosa. 30 These occlusions are usually caused by remote embolization from the ipsilateral internal carotid artery, or they may originate from the aortic arch or the heart. This conclusion is supported by a study of 234 patients with NA‐CRAO in which 85% of patients underwent cervical vascular imaging, of which 71% exhibited ipsilateral carotid plaque. Only 18% of the study's patients had >80% carotid artery stenosis, suggesting that hemodynamic impairment was not a likely cause of CRAO presentation in those patients. 3 The recovery in patients was found to be dependent on the retinal tolerance time, which is the capacity of the retina to heal after an ischemic event, and the time it took to resolve the thrombus/embolus. 23 , 31 NA‐CRAO is subdivided into 3 further subgroups, which are highlighted in detail in Table 1.

caption.p: Summary of CRAO Subtypes and the Associated Pathophysiological Features, Incidence, and Outcomes

tr.td: Classic nonarteritic CRAO

Caused by platelet fibrin thrombi and emboli as a result of atherosclerotic disease, mostly originating from plaques in the heart or carotid artery. Emboli impaction commonly occurs where the CRA enters the optic nerve sheath because of its narrower lumen here. Some more rare causes include vasculitis, autoimmune diseases, and thrombophilia.
Accounts for over two‐thirds of all CRAO cases

After adjusted for age and sex, the incidence is 1.9 per 100 000 person‐years in the United States, 1.8 per 100 000 person‐years in South Korea, and 2.5 per 100 000 person‐years in Japan. In the population aged >80 years, the incidence increases to 10.1 per 100 000 person‐years. A total of 95% are of the nonarteritic subtypes, whereas only 5% are arteritic.
In the first 7 days following event, vision sometimes improves (22%), usually remains the same (66%), or worsens (12%).
Nonarteritic CRAO with cilioretinal artery sparing
Same as classic nonarteritic CRAO, but with a patent cilioretinal artery present, which results in preserved perfusion of the macula region and is protective for complete vision loss in patients.
Accounts for a small subset of nonarteritic CRAOs.
In the first 7 days following the event, vision usually improves (67%) or stays the same (33%).
Transient nonarteritic CRAO

Also known as transient monocular blindness. Caused by a migrating embolus, but also caused by a transient decrease of perfusion pressure or elevated intraocular pressure. Transient vasospasm attributable to serotonin release from platelets on atherosclerotic plaques has also been suggested as a mechanism of transient CRAO in animal models.
Accounts for 15% of CRAOs.

In the first 7 days following the event, vision usually improves (82%) or stays the same (18%). However, people experiencing this disease have a 1 % risk per year of having a permanent nonarteritic CRAO.
Arteritic CRAO

This condition is caused by inflammatory vascular causes, including giant cell arteritis, polyarteritis nodosa, granulomatosis polyangiitis, and SLE (vascular complication), secondary to inflammatory thrombosis of the common trunk of the posterior ciliary artery and CRA.
Accounts for <5% of CRAOs.
In the first 7 days following the event, vision shows no change.

fn.p: CRA indicates central retinal artery; CRAO, CRA occlusion; and SLE, systemic lupus erythematosus.

sec.p: The arteritic subtype of CRAO, which is less prevalent than the thromboembolic subtype, involves an inflammatory process as the primary mechanism for occluding arteries (Table 1). One of the main mechanisms by which vasculitis causes blockage of arteries is through the formation of immune deposits. 6 , 32 , 33 , 34 , 35 In immune complex vasculitis, circulating immune complexes can deposit in the walls of blood vessels, leading to inflammation and damage to the vessel walls. 6 , 32 , 33 , 36 , 37 , 38 , 39 These immune deposits can also activate complement, leading to further inflammation and damage. 33 As a result of this inflammatory process, the blood vessel walls can become thickened and narrowed, leading to blockage or occlusion. 32 , 33 , 34 , 35 In addition, vasculitides are known for endothelial damage, which may result in activation of platelets and consequent formation of thrombi that occlude the blood vessels. 33 Causes of arteritic CRAO reported in literature include GCA, polyarteritis nodosa, granulomatosis with polyangiitis, Susac syndrome, and systemic lupus erythematosus. Polyarteritis nodosa may cause endothelial inflammation and consequent blockage of medium‐sized vessels in the body. 40 In granulomatosis with polyangiitis and microscopic polyangiitis, the inflammation can lead to the formation of granulomas, which are collections of immune cells and debris. 33 , 41 , 42 Susac syndrome is a rare autoimmune disorder that affects the small blood vessels of the brain, retina, and inner ear. 43 , 44 In Susac syndrome, inflammation and occlusion of the small blood vessels in the retina can cause CRAO, leading to vision loss. 43 , 44 Systemic lupus erythematosus is a systemic autoimmune disease, with vasculitis being a common manifestation, leading to retinal artery occlusion secondary to immune deposits. 37 , 38 , 39 The associated systemic inflammation in these disease processes also creates a state of hypercoagulability, which increases the overall risk of infarction and ischemic events. 33

The most frequent cause of arteritic CRAO is GCA, which results in inflammatory cell–mediated lumen‐stenosing intimal hyperplasia of the distal branches of the carotid artery and other medium and large extracranial arteries. 8 , 32 , 45 Because of this vasculitic blockage of the posterior ciliary arteries, eyes with GCA‐induced CRAO may also develop concurrent arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION) and choroidal ischemia. 8 It is possible for the cilioretinal artery to become occluded because of arteritic blockage of the posterior ciliary arteries in GCA, which manifests as GCA‐associated cilioretinal artery occlusion. In a large group of patients with biopsy‐proven GCA, 8.2% of patients had permanent vision loss. Of these patients, 6.9% had AION, 1.6% had CRAO, and 0.4% had cilioretinal artery blockage. 46 The inner and outer retina as well as the optic nerve head may simultaneously become ischemic because of inflammation of the proximal ophthalmic artery in GCA. This was also observed in the study by Chen et al, where 1 patient (0.4%) had both CRAO and a contralateral AION, and 1 patient (0.4%) had both CRAO and an AION in the same eye. 46 If arteritic cause is suspected, it is critical to evaluate the inflammatory markers and administer systemic corticosteroids immediately.

In addition, CRAO may also present as a symptom of various inflammatory and infectious systemic illnesses, including autoimmune vasculitis, which need to be treated accordingly to resolve the insult. Rarely, a hypercoagulable condition may coexist and lead to CRA thrombosis. 36 Finally, in rare cases, cosmetic facial injections may result in iatrogenic CRAO if a synthetic substance is accidentally injected into the facial arteries that collateralize with the ophthalmic artery. 47 The prognosis in these situations is typically poor, and because the obstruction is not fibrin based, thrombolysis therapy may not be appropriate.

sec.title: Physical Examination and Diagnosis
sec.p: During the clinical evaluation of patients with CRAO, 5 factors are usually assessed: history of monocular visual loss, clinical risk factors, an ocular investigation, general physical examination, and necessary ancillary examination to confirm or rule out other differential diagnoses 10 , 11 (summarized in Figure 2).

caption.p: A summarized algorithm of assessments of patients with suspected central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO). 18 CRA indicates central retinal artery; and GCA, giant cell arteritis.

sec.title: Vision Loss
sec.p: The visual loss usually associated with CRAO is painless, but sudden and rapid. 10 , 11 , 48 However, in the few days following the initial onset of CRAO, there is an improvement in visual acuity in most patients. 7 Impaired color vision is usually diminished to a level proportional to the visual acuity defect. 14 The specific defects are the monocular loss of light perception and an afferent pupillary defect; meanwhile, extraocular eye movements, IOPs, and the anterior chamber examination results will all present as normal. 11

sec.title: Ocular Examination
sec.p: Most CRAO cases, up to 90%, will show a “cherry‐red spot” on fundoscopy. 10 , 19 Other common findings in a study of 240 patients with CRAO were retinal opacity in the posterior pole, pallor, retina arterial attenuation, optic disk edema, and “box caring,” or segmentation of the arterioles. 19 , 49 In later progressions of CRAO, almost all patients showed optic atrophy. 19 A fundoscopic examination is essential to completing the diagnosis of CRAO; however, a dilated eye examination should also be performed when tolerated by patients. 11

sec.title: Risk Factors and Physical Examination
sec.p: CRAO is associated with some physical risk factors, which makes it crucial to thoroughly investigate medical history of each patient presenting with the relevant clinical signs. 14 For example, men are known to have a rate of incidence 1.47 times higher than women. 50 , 51 Age also increases the risk of CRAO exponentially; 1 study found an incidence rate of 10.08 per 100 000 people in those aged 80 to 84 years. 50 Before diagnosis, clinical risk factors, like a family history of cerebrovascular and/or cardiovascular disease, are assessed. 10 , 14 One study found 67% of patients with CRAO had at least 1 cardiovascular risk factor. 14 Cardiovascular risk factors, including obesity, hypertension, tobacco use, high cholesterol, and diabetes, all showed high prevalence in patients with CRAO. 51 In fact, on diagnosis of CRAO, 21 of 33 patients were diagnosed with a vascular risk factor; hyperlipidemia was the most common, followed by hypertension. 12 Patients’ radial pulse rate should be evaluated as irregular rhythms can be indicative of atrial fibrillation, a risk for embolic events. 10 Again, signs of hypertension are also critical for investigation as CRAO is highly associated with cardiovascular irregularities. 10 Also, uncommon risk factors of the disease are being explored. Conditions, like sickle cell disease, vasculitis, and even the use of oral contraceptive pills, could become an important part of CRAO examination and diagnosis. 10 , 52

sec.title: Other Considerations
sec.p: In the short‐term phase, fundus fluorescein angiography usually reveals normal choroidal filling with delayed or absent filling of the CRA. If the choroidal circulation is also impaired, especially in patients aged >50 years with CRAO, no visible retinal emboli, and presenting with waxy pallor of the optic disk, then GCA must be ruled out and other arteritic causes of CRAO may also be considered, depending on patient history and presentation. 1 , 4 , 10 , 14

Moreover, when considering inflammatory and arteritic causes of CRAO an evaluation of erythrocyte sedimentation rate and CRP (C‐reactive protein) can be helpful; however the low specificity of these tests calls for further investigation with patient history, physical examination, obtaining further inflammatory markers (including antineutrophilic cytoplasmic antibody, antinuclear antibody, complement levels, anti–double‐stranded DNA, myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibodies, serum protein electrophoresis, coagulation studies, HIV, and syphilis serologies to evaluate various retinopathies and vasculitis), potential arterial imaging (magnetic resonance imaging/magnetic resonance angiography of head and neck in most cases), and finally biopsy to confirm diagnosis are required. 34 , 35 , 38 , 42 , 53 , 54 , 55 , 56 During the physical examination, scalp tenderness should be assessed along with other constellation of symptoms that are indicative of giant cell (temporal) arteritis, including polymyalgia, polyarthralgia, jaw claudication, and aortic aneurysm versus dissection. 32 , 46 , 57 , 58 Patients who present with high levels of inflammation and other symptoms of GCA should be given high‐dose corticosteroids to prevent contralateral eye vision loss. 4 , 11 For patients with symptom onset of <6 hours, a compute tomography head scan is justifiable to eliminate the possibility of an intracranial hemorrhage (high diagnostic accuracy), which would also determine if they are candidates for thrombolytic therapies. 11 , 59 , 60

sec.title: Differential Diagnoses
sec.p: Differential diagnoses to rule out during workup include acute angle‐close glaucoma, AION, globe rupture, retinal detachment, retinal vein occlusion, vitreous hemorrhage, and other conditions. 11 , 61 , 62 Because various conditions can present with monocular vision loss, it is important to distinguish between these causes. For example, transient blindness, similar to that of CRAO, may be experienced in patients who are experiencing ophthalmic artery vasospasm; however, the cherry‐red spot on a fundus examination that defines CRAO will not be present. 63

Acute angle‐closure glaucoma can cause transient unilateral disturbances in the eye; however, it is usually painful and associated with redness in the eye. 61 Any orbital tumor that creates pressure on the optic nerve can cause vision loss in the affected eye. However, usually, vision loss will only be present when looking in a certain direction. 61 Finally, a monocular vision loss that is precipitated by postural changes is most likely associated with papilledema. 53 , 61

The rate and aftermath of visual loss can provide insight into cause. In patients with optic neuropathy, the visual loss may be sudden but fairly static and show no improvement. 62 Unilateral optic neuropathy can be confirmed by the relative afferent pupillary defect. The relative afferent pupillary defect is determined by the swinging flashlight test. 62 Patients with optic neuropathy and optic disk swelling or hemorrhage may be presenting with nonarteritic ischemic optic neuropathy, GCA, or other inflammatory conditions. 62 Neuropathy with a normal disk may be indicative of optic neuritis, neuromyelitis optica, or Leber hereditary optic neuropathy. 62

Although an important characteristic of CRAO is the cherry‐red spot found on fundoscopy, this is also present in other conditions, like Tay‐Sachs and Niemann‐Pick disease. 11 , 64 Tay‐Sachs disease presents differently than CRAO as the onset of symptoms occurs in infancy. 64 , 65 However, children with this neurodegenerative disorder do show a cherry‐red macular spot in both eyes. 43 Niemann‐Pick disease is the result of a mutation in the NPC2 gene, and ≈50% of cases show a cherry‐red spot in the macula. The symptoms of this disorder extend beyond the ocular manifestations, and thus it should be readily distinguishable from other disorders. Leukemic infiltrates into the head of the optic nerve may cause CRAO and cherry‐red spots. 66 Macular infarction from trauma, retinal vascular occlusion, lupus, and sickle‐cell anemia may present with cherry‐red spots in the macular region as well and shall be differentiated clinically by evaluating patient history and symptom manifestations thoroughly. 67

sec.title: Clinical Management of CRAO
sec.p: There is currently insufficient evidence in the literature on management and treatment efficacy for CRAO. 14 However, an evidence‐based management pathway has been established by the American Heart Association that is constantly modified with the latest evidence. 14 Timing between insult to presentation is critical for determining management strategy and, therefore, treatment should begin immediately before starting the workup.

sec.title: Conventional Treatments
sec.p: There are several standard treatments that are often used in combination. These treatments and maneuvers are aimed at quickly lowering the IOP, which allows the occlusion to move more distally through the vessel (Table 2). More important, arteritic AION (GCA) should always be considered as a differential diagnosis and, if suspected, immediate systemic corticosteroids with slow taper over a year are indicated (this treatment may be stopped as indicated during further workup). 4 , 11 , 68

caption.p: Common Treatment Methods for Management of CRAO

tr.td: Intraocular pressure lowering (topical β‐blocker, carbonic anhydrase inhibitor, α‐agonist, or anterior chamber paracentesis) 23 , 70 , 71
Decreased intraocular pressure to increase perfusion pressures and dislodge obstruction
Risk of recurrence
Ocular massage 72
Retinal artery dilatation and fluctuations in intraocular pressure
Risk of embolus moving peripherally and worsening symptoms
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy 72
Increased oxygen delivery and subsequent assisted reperfusion
May exacerbate preexisting macular edema
Intravenous tPA 14 , 25
Naturally occurring fibrinolytic to dissolve clot
Reduced efficacy after 4.5 h
Intra‐arterial thrombolysis 80 , 81 , 82 , 83
Functions locally to dissolve clot
Careful patient selection because of increased risk of adverse events

fn.p: CRAO indicates central retinal artery occlusion; and tPA, tissue‐type plasminogen activator.

sec.p: By decreasing IOP, ocular perfusion increases. This reduction in IOP can be achieved with various known drugs, including systemic acetazolamide (Diamox; 500 mg), mannitol, and topical timolol (hypotensive eyedrops), or through anterior chamber paracentesis. Anterior chamber paracentesis is the process of aqueous humor drainage from the anterior chamber. It is usually performed if the CRAO is <24 hours and the individual is not a candidate for intra‐arterial thrombolysis (IAT). A limitation of anterior chamber paracentesis is that it only takes 1 to 2 hours for a deflated anterior chamber to return to normotensive range. Once reinflated, the blockage tends to recur. A retrospective review of 50 patients who received anterior chamber paracentesis as a treatment for CRAO showed that the patients had no improvement in visual acuity. 69 Therefore, IOP reduction strategies as monotherapy are generally ineffective. 23 , 70 , 71

Ocular massage consists of intermittent digital pressure applied to the affected eye to allow aqueous fluid to manually flow out. When this digital pressure is released, it causes the IOP to lower, increasing retinal perfusion pressure. 72 However, a complication to this form of treatment is that the retinal embolus can potentially move downstream and worsen symptoms. The incidence of this complication and its associated mechanisms require further investigation.

Hyperbaric oxygen therapy has been found to be an effective treatment for NA‐CRAO as long as a cherry‐red spot, attributable to retinal edema, is not formed on the macula. During this therapy, the patient inhales 100% oxygen at pressures >1 atmospheric pressure. This increases the amount of oxygen dissolved in the plasma 20 to 30 times as the arterial oxygen tension exceeds 2000 mm Hg. The oxygen delivery to the ischemic tissue occurs until spontaneous or assisted reperfusion is achieved. In 1 study, 67.2% of the patients had clinically significant visual improvement. 73 In this study, 7 of the 128 patients did experience adverse effects, which included mild barotrauma, otalgia, and myringotomy. 73

Given that CRAO and cerebral ischemic stroke share underlying mechanisms, treatment may also include secondary prevention of a recurrent cerebrovascular event. In line with the recommendations of the American Heart Association/American Stroke Association Stroke Prevention 2021 Guidelines, 74 this includes both lifestyle modifications and medical therapy. Lifestyle modifications include improved blood pressure management, glycemic control, weight loss, increased physical activity, and healthier diets, such as the Mediterranean diet. Medical therapy type and duration depend on factors such as the patient's age, medical comorbidities, and stroke severity. A reasonable regimen shown to be most effective in the literature for secondary stroke prevention is a short‐term combination of aspirin and clopidogrel for 21 to 90 days, followed by long‐term therapy with either aspirin or clopidogrel alone. 74 , 75 , 76 , 77 , 78 However, antiplatelet therapy following CRAO has not been thoroughly investigated, and further study may change the way secondary prevention is approached. One retrospective study of 9437 patients with CRAO found that most recurrent cerebrovascular accidents occurred in the first week following CRAO. 79 This supports the use of short‐term dual‐antiplatelet therapy. However, in a propensity score–matched analysis of patients who did versus did not receive aspirin following CRAO, this study found no significant difference in risk of ischemic stroke or additional safety outcomes, including bleeding or ocular symptoms, at 1‐year follow‐up. 79

sec.title: Endovascular Treatments
sec.p: IAT has been described, but its benefit is not proven and requires further study. Local administration of thrombolytic agents has the advantage of working near the occlusion, which enables the titration of the minimally effective dose to achieve revascularization. This is achieved through endovascular thrombolytic administration in the proximal ophthalmic artery, requiring a trained proceduralist and ophthalmologist for a thorough postprocedure examination. Some studies have suggested that early reperfusion rates were significantly greater in groups receiving IAT than in those receiving standard treatment. 80 , 81 , 82 , 83 In these studies, standard treatments included ocular massage, anterior chamber paracentesis, IOP‐lowering agents, hyperbaric oxygen, anticoagulants, and hemodilution. A total of 84.6% of patients with incomplete CRAO in the IAT group had clinically significant visual improvement on their final checkup, whereas only 14.3% of patients improved when they had a subtotal CRAO, and none improved when they had a total CRAO. 80 , 84 This highlights the prognostic importance of accurately diagnosing the extent of occlusion in CRAO, as patients with incomplete CRAO respond better to IAT. Major complications associated with IAT include ischemic stroke (diffusion weighted imaging findings), as well as groin and intracranial bleeding. 80 , 84 None described in the literature has been shown to lead to long‐term neurologic deficit. 84 Minor complications include headache and dizziness. Complications are common with this approach, as was evident by the EAGLE (European Assessment Group for Lysis in the Eye) trial. 51 This trial enrolled 84 patients to receive either IAT or standard medical therapy and was prematurely terminated for futility after no significant difference was shown in the primary outcome (corrected visual acuity by day 30), with significantly increased rate of adverse events in the IAT group (37.1% versus 4.3%). This could be in part attributable to the failure to administer this therapy rapidly, with an average time to treatment of 13 hours following symptom onset. However, quantitative pooled analysis of all studies testing efficacy of IAT did find a significantly higher rate of corrected visual acuity in the IAT group when compared with the standard therapy group, when analyzing both with and without including the EAGLE trial. 84 This implies that time to intervention is critical for patient outcomes, and that IAT within 6 hours is a feasible intervention for CRAO. 14 , 84 Recently, in a systematic review of the literature by Page et al, it was found that the mean time from last known normal to treatment with IAT for CRAO was 9.51 hours (range, 1–172 hours). 84 Perhaps, with reduced time to diagnosis and easier access to an equipped interventional suite, this approach may become the standard of care. The current data imply that with faster intervention, IAT is a promising approach, with potentially reduced adverse effects compared with intravenous thrombolysis, although early identification is likely critical to improve clinical outcomes.

Intravenous tPA is used in patients within 4.5 hours of the incident if there is no intracranial or systemic hemorrhage. Alteplase is the most used agent, which is delivered via intravenous infusion. A patient‐level meta‐analysis of observational studies found that patients with acute CRAO who were treated with any fibrinolytic drug had a 62% rate of clinical recovery when treated within 4.5 hours of onset. 24 In 1 study of 112 patients, 25 were treated with intravenous alteplase. A total of 44% of the alteplase‐treated patients had recovery of visual acuity when treated within 4.5 hours versus 13.1% of patients who did recover without alteplase. 25 The treatment has reduced efficacy if the tPA is administered after 4.5 hours as none of the patients treated with alteplase between 4.5 and 6 hours after symptom onset demonstrated recovery. 14 , 25 This was also demonstrated by Schrag et al, who observed a decrease in the rate of clinical recovery from 62% to 17% when time to therapy increased from <4.5 to between 4.5 and 12 hours following symptom onset. 24 This approach is likely only effective in patients with CRAO attributable to fibrin‐based clot versus a calcium‐based embolus. Nedelmann et al demonstrated, in 11 patients who received tPA, that all 7 who had a retrobulbar spot sign (indicative of likely calcium embolus in CRA) showed persistent visual impairment at final follow‐up. 85 Thus, accurate identification of a fibrin‐based embolus is key when considering thrombolytic therapy. On the basis of these data, intravenous fibrinolytics improve outcomes when administered within 4.5 hours of CRAO, but better selection of patients is needed to balance risks with potential benefits.

sec.title: Emerging Diagnostic and Treatment Paradigms
sec.p: Because of the lack of standardized treatment for CRAO, prospective clinical trials are urgently needed to guide practice. Delays in presentation make identifying the retinal penumbra critical to guide treatment decisions. The treatment for arteritic CRAO (eg, in GCA) is immediate initiation of high‐dose corticosteroids and management of the vasculitis. 68 In the upcoming sections, we focus and discuss emerging diagnostic/imaging strategies as well as novel treatments for NA‐CRAO being tested clinically and preclinically (algorithm summarized in Figure 3).

caption.p: Algorithm summarizing current understanding of proposed clinical approach toward treatment of central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO), with treatment initiation within 4.5‐hour window (currently being studied in clinical trials). IA indicates intra‐arterial; IV, intravenous; OCT‐A, optical coherence tomography angiography; PAM+SD‐OCT, photoacoustic microscopy+spectral‐domain optical coherence tomography; and RFI, retinal functional imaging.

sec.title: Clinical Interventions
sec.p: There are currently 10 “active,” “currently recruiting,” or “not yet recruiting” trials listed on clinicaltrials.gov with the term retinal artery occlusion associated with them. Of these, 4 of 10 are investigating fibrinolysis using either alteplase or tenecteplase, which has greater fibrin specificity and a longer half‐life than alteplase. 86 Study NCT04965038 is a randomized controlled clinical trial evaluating the effect of intravenous alteplase in CRAO within 4.5 hours of symptom onset. Similarly, studies NCT04526951 and NCT03197194 are randomized controlled phase 3 trials of intravenous tenecteplase and alteplase, respectively, with aspirin or placebo tablet for patients with CRAO within 4.5 hours of symptom onset. Study NCT05562284 is investigating the effect of intra‐arterial administration of alteplase within 7 days of CRAO on visual field deficits. Notably, the 7‐day time frame is significantly longer than the ≤4.5 hours expected survival time of retinal tissue predicted by preclinical studies. 87 , 88 , 89

A major limitation of previous studies for fibrinolysis in CRAO is delayed presentation, with patients presenting >24 hours following onset of symptoms. In a recent study published in 2021, >60% of patients in the study presented after 24 hours, which results in lack of timely appropriate workup and delivery of appropriate treatments for these patients. 90 Increasing public awareness of CRAO by changing the stroke acronym “FAST” to “BE‐FAST” (balance, eyes, face, arm, speech, and time) may facilitate timely intervention with fibrinolytics and potentially better outcomes. 26 , 89 In addition, the 3 randomized controlled trials described will hopefully provide insight into whether fibrinolysis within the initial 4.5‐hour window improves outcomes in CRAO as it does in cerebral stroke.

sec.title: Clinical Imaging
sec.p: Fibrinolysis is a promising therapy for CRAO, but currently it is unclear which patients will benefit from fibrinolysis. 88 It is likely that patients who present early and/or have significant collateral circulation will benefit more from fibrinolytic therapy. 13 , 24 Better evaluating the “retinal penumbra,” or area of retinal tissue that is damaged but salvageable if reperfused, in patients with CRAO could further guide fibrinolytic therapy. 13 Recent studies in cerebral stroke indicate that patients with evidence of salvageable brain tissue receiving fibrinolysis up to 9 hours after symptom onset have fewer neurologic deficits, highlighting the potential of perfusion imaging to guide fibrinolytic therapy. 91 , 92 Of the 10 trials on clinicaltrials.gov, 4 are investigating imaging of the retina.

NCT01348633 is testing Doppler spectral‐domain optical coherence tomography (SD‐OCT) for generating oxygen saturation maps of the retina. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is an interferometry‐based noninvasive imaging modality with a resolution on the order of 10 μm, making it ideal for imaging retinal tissue. 13 , 93 , 94 SD‐OCT has increased tissue discrimination and less artifact than traditional OCT, and SD‐OCT findings, such as retinal thickening, have been identified as indicative of retinal ischemia. 9 , 95 A retrospective study of 12 patients with CRAO identified progressive retinal edema on SD‐OCT over time with an estimated progression rate of 10 μm/h of ischemia, suggesting SD‐OCT may be useful for approximating ischemic duration in patients with no last known normal. 96 A follow‐up retrospective study of 66 patients with CRAO used relative retinal thickness index on SD‐OCT imaging to identify patients presenting within the first 4.5 hours of CRAO with 100% sensitivity. 97 NCT05130385 is investigating a high‐resolution OCT system with a reported resolution of 3 μm for its ability to image retinal fluid in numerous ophthalmologic conditions, including CRAO. 98 Increased resolution may improve the correlation of retinal thickness with time of ischemia. Thus, SD‐OCT represents a promising method to identify patients who may benefit from fibrinolytic therapy by estimating the time of retinal ischemia.

NCT02141308 is evaluating the ability of OCT angiography (OCT‐A) to quantify retinal blood flow. OCT‐A uses OCT to differentiate between areas of tissue with erythrocyte movement and nonvascular tissue. 99 However, current OCT‐A techniques cannot differentiate low‐ and high‐flow tissue segments, and methods to accurately quantify blood flow are still being investigated. 13 , 100 , 101 , 102 Development and validation of methods to quantify blood flow using OCT‐A could provide information about collateral vascularization in patients with CRAO, potentially offering a technique to risk stratify patients for fibrinolytic therapy. Patients with CRAO with low‐flow rates may have a lower likelihood of benefiting from fibrinolysis compared with patients with higher flow rates. Advancements in quantitative OCT‐A are promising for predicting the presence of viable retinal tissue following CRAO.

NCT03475173 is using laser speckle flowgraphy to evaluate and quantify retinal blood flow in patients with branch retinal artery occlusion. Laser speckle imaging leverages differences in static tissue and fluid interference to generate flow velocity maps. 13 , 103 Laser speckle imaging has been used to quantify retinal blood flow in healthy volunteers and patients with glaucoma. 104 , 105 , 106 Results from this study in branch retinal artery occlusion will be useful for determining the utility of laser speckle flowgraphy in risk stratifying patients with CRAO for fibrinolytic therapy Table 2).

Currently, numerous imaging techniques are being evaluated to determine either collateral blood flow or duration of ischemia in CRAO (summarized in Table 3). Duration of ischemia has been shown to correlate with viability of retinal tissue in animal CRAO model tissue and brain tissue in human patients with stroke. 87 , 88 , 107 , 108 Increasingly, tissue perfusion measurements, rather than time of symptoms, are being used to guide fibrinolysis decisions in stroke care. 91 , 92 , 109 Effectively identifying the retinal penumbra in patients with CRAO with the imaging techniques previously described would likely improve patient selection and outcomes for fibrinolytic therapy.

sec.title: Preclinical Data
sec.p: Low incidence and variable clinical presentations make prospective clinical trials difficult to perform in CRAO; thus, preclinical data could be invaluable for identifying promising new therapies. 113 , 114 A wide variety of animal models have been used, but the translatability of these models can be questionable. 115 An excellent review of the various animals and methods of inducing retinal artery occlusion is described by Vestergaard et al. 115 Briefly, rodents and monkeys are the most used animals. Of these, monkeys are likely better for translatability of findings because they have a similar retinal vasculature to humans; however, certain techniques, such as endovascular procedures, are easier to perform on larger animals, like pigs. 115 Pigs have relatively similar retinal vasculature to humans, but critically there is debate about the presence of a central artery. Of the various methods to induce artery occlusion in animal models, techniques that specifically occlude the retinal artery without damaging nearby structures are the most like the pathophysiological features in patients. 115 Of the described methods, ligation/clamping and laser‐induced occlusion appear the most appropriate. With these limitations in mind, we will discuss novel therapeutics and imaging techniques that warrant further investigation.

Neuroprotective agents that reduce retinal ganglion cell death following CRAO could improve visual outcomes, especially at time points when fibrinolysis would not benefit patients. A phase 1/2 study demonstrated that intravitreal injection of KUS121, an ATPase inhibitor of VCP (valosin‐containing protein), between 4 and 48 hours of CRAO symptom onset was safe and significantly improved visual acuity outcomes compared with historical controls. 116 The authors had previously shown that KUS121 suppresses endoplasmic reticulum stress to reduce cell death in ischemic rat retinal ganglion cells. 117 The inclusion of patients up to 48 hours in this preliminary study is promising, because the current view, analogous to stroke data, 10 is that fibrinolytic therapy is unlikely to benefit patients with CRAO beyond 9 hours. A phase 3 trial will be necessary to definitively evaluate the efficacy of this neuroprotective agent in CRAO. In a mouse model of retinal ischemic reperfusion injury, Lei et al demonstrated that administration of rasagiline, a neuroprotective agent, and idebenone, an antioxidant, in combination reduced retinal ganglion cell apoptosis. 118 Reperfusion injury is important in the natural history of retinal artery occlusion, and after administration of fibrinolytics, it can be observed in patients with stroke. 119 , 120 Reperfusion injury may also contribute to the variable clinical efficacy of fibrinolysis in CRAO. Thus, neuroprotective agents offer an additional avenue for clinical improvement following CRAO.

Novel imaging techniques that measure oxygen use in retinal tissue would also aid in evaluation of the retinal penumbra. Photoacoustic microscopy uses a pulse of optical energy to induce ultrasonic emission and can be used to image the retina with hemoglobin as endogenous contrast. 13 , 121 Photoacoustic microscopy was combined with SD‐OCT to map the retinal metabolic oxygen rate in rats. 110 If translated to patients with CRAO, this strategy could help differentiate retinal infarcted tissue from the retinal penumbra.

Another method of evaluating retinal oxygen use is retinal functional imaging. Retinal functional imaging provides information on local capillary perfusion, retinal oxygenation, blood flow velocity, and retinal metabolism. 13 , 112 Retinal functional imaging has been used in humans to evaluate retinal blood flow in various pathologic conditions, including retinal vein occlusion. 111 Retinal functional imaging could be another promising imaging modality for measuring retinal tissue viability and guiding fibrinolysis decision‐making.

The translation of these imaging technologies to the clinic could improve patient selection for fibrinolytic therapy. In addition, investigating the efficacy of novel neuroprotective drugs, like KUS121, is essential for patients who cannot receive fibrinolytic therapy. Advancements in both these areas of investigation should increase the therapeutic window of fibrinolysis and improve outcomes in patients with CRAO.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: In this review, we have established that CRAO is an urgent ophthalmologic condition that leads to acquired blindness in patients. Our current understanding of its pathophysiological features and the therapeutic approach for clinical management is comparable to the underlying mechanisms applied in cerebral ischemic stroke. To date, long treatment windows are just one of the factors that have hampered clinical management trials using intravenous tPA for CRAO. Newer trials exploring the role of intra‐arterial tPA and hyperbaric oxygen therapy have shown promise, but they have their own limitations. Moreover, conventional therapeutic options have demonstrated minimal effect on visual outcomes. Hence, future studies focusing on the development and implementation of improved thrombolytic therapies, earlier treatment windows, using novel imaging techniques to incorporate endovascular strategies, and incorporating novel neuroprotectants for clinical management in conjunction with recanalization therapy show promise in improving management and outcomes of patients with CRAO.
